coredata.dc:title: Determination of dopamine by a label-free fluorescent aptasensor based on AuNPs and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Linjiao
Hang, Xinxin
Qin, Zirui
Zhang, Pei
Wang, Wei
Zhang, Yintang
Jiang, Liying
coredata.dc:description: In order to reduce the complex modification process and obtain a fast and label-free aptasensor, a label-free fluorescent aptasensor based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was constructed for rapid determination of dopamine (DA). In the absence of DA, CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs through the electrostatic force between CQDs and aptamer, which led to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. When DA were added, they combined with the corresponding aptamers with higher affinity and selectivity, resulting in CQDs far from the surface of AuNPs and thus the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was recovered. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of the released CQDs was linearly proportional to the concentration of DA in a range of 5×10-8˜2.5×10-4mol/L with a detection limit of 1×10-8mol/L. Moreover, the aptasensor exhibited a satisfactory detection result of DA in human serum samples. This study provided a more effective method to detect a broad range of targets by using appropriate aptamers, holding great potential in the field of food safety and biomedical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Label-free
Fluorescent aptasensor
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles

Detection of DA in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61801436 ), Science and Technology Innovation Outstanding Talents Project of Henan Province (Grant No. 184200510015 ), Henan Province Science and Technology Plan Project - International Cooperation Project (Grant No. 182102410093 ), Key research projects in colleges and universities of Henan Province (Grant No. 18A510021 ), Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing ( HKLBRSK1801 ) and Graduate Technology Innovation Fund Project of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry (Grant No. 2018022 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs(a), CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer(b), and CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA(c).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA, when the volumes of aptamer (10 μM) were set as 5, 20, 30 and 45 μL, and the AuNPs, CQDs and DA were unchanged. (b) The relationship between peak fluorescence intensity (495 nm) and the volume of aptamer.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer when the volumes of CQDs were changed from 60 μL to 300 μL. (b) The relationship between peak fluorescence intensity (495 nm) and the volume of CQDs.
(a) the fluorescence spectra of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA system with different concentrations (0.05, 25, 100, 150, 200, 250 μM) of DA, Fig. 4(b) calibration curve of DA concentration signal intensity.
The specific detection of DA hydrochloride (25 μM).

(a)the fluorescence spectra of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA system with different concentrations (0.8、25、40、60 and 100 μM) of DA in human serum samples, (b) calibration curve of DA concentration signal intensity.
Schematic of the DA detection based on AuNPs and carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Determination of dopamine by a label-free fluorescent aptasensor based on AuNPs and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electric Information Engineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electric Information Engineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu, 476000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHenan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu, 476000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to reduce the complex modification process and obtain a fast and label-free aptasensor, a label-free fluorescent aptasensor based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was constructed for rapid determination of dopamine (DA). In the absence of DA, CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs through the electrostatic force between CQDs and aptamer, which led to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. When DA were added, they combined with the corresponding aptamers with higher affinity and selectivity, resulting in CQDs far from the surface of AuNPs and thus the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was recovered. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescence intensity of the released CQDs was linearly proportional to the concentration of DA in a range of 5×10-8˜2.5×10-4mol/L with a detection limit of 1×10-8mol/L. Moreover, the aptasensor exhibited a satisfactory detection result of DA in human serum samples. This study provided a more effective method to detect a broad range of targets by using appropriate aptamers, holding great potential in the field of food safety and biomedical diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Aptamers have been widely investigated in detecting many biomolecules due to their high affinity and specificity [1–3]. When aptamers are used as sensors need to be modified with labeling fluorophore, because of aptamers could not produce any light signals [4]. Nowadays, fluorescent aptasensor have been widely researched for biomolecular detection in clinical diagnostics and food safety fields, because of the high sensitivity from fluorescence detection and the high sensitivity according to the nucleic acid aptamer [5,6].

Most fluorescent aptasensor use organic dyes as fluorophores such as rhodamine B [7], calcein blue Fe2+ complex [8], FAM [9], Cy3 [10], and so on. However, these fluorescent groups often have negative effects on human health [11]. Humans are carbon-based creatures. Compared to traditional organic dyes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have many advantages in biosensing, such as better photostability [12], lower toxicity [13] and better biocompatibility [14]. Combining the aptamers and CQDs, a fluorescent aptasensor with low toxicity would be constructed. However, the experimental process of modifying carbon quantum dots to fixed positions on nucleic acids is complex [15]. The surface of naked carbon quantum contains large amounts of hydroxyl groups [16], which is easy for DNA molecules to bond to through hydrogen bonds. Therefore, to simplify the experiment, a label-free fluorescent aptasensor based on gold nano-solution (AuNPs) and CQDs was constructed for rapid determination of biological molecules.

Abnormal concentrations of dopamine (DA) are associated with various diseases and cancers. It is urgent to control the quantitative DA for disease diagonsis and prognosis [17–19]. In this study, DA can be used as a probe to study the chemical reactivity and performance of the sensor. The scheme of this label-free fluorescent aptasensor was shown in Scheme 1 . Wherein, CQDs and AuNPs were served as fluorescent donor-quencher pair, and aptamer was DA recognition group. In the absence of DA, CQDs were adsorbed on the aptamer-AuNPs through electrostatic force between CQDs and aptamer, and the fluorescence of CQDs is quenched by AuNPs. When DA was added, dopamine specifically combined with its aptamer, resulted in CQDs far from AuNPs, which lead to the fluorescence recovery of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity was linearly related to the concentration of target DA under appropriate conditions. The reaction time takes only a few minutes, which indicates the rapidity of this developed sensor for DA detection. Moreover, the label-free sensor requiring no modification of nanomaterials or aptamer has good response characteristics in both PBS buffer and human serum sample, which make it a wide application prospects in the field of food safety and biomedical diagnostics.

Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu), urea and dopamine aptamer were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 20 nm AuNps purchased from Shanghai JieYi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Na+ (NaCl), Mg2+ (MgCl2), amino acid (AA), ATP and sucrose were purchased from Zhengzhou Xinmaijie Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). Dopamine aptamer:5′- GTC TCT GTG TGC GCC AGA GAA CAC TGG GGC AGA TAT GGG CCA GCA CAG AAT GAG GCC C-(CH2)3-SH-3′ [20]. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) and human serum were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS,10 mM, pH 7.4), Tris−HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 5.2). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and the ultrapure water used throughout the study was prepared by PURELAB Option-R (ELGA LabWater, UK).

Fluorescence measurements were performed on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The ultrasonication during the preparation of CQDs was performed on a SYU-22-500DTD ultrasonic cleaner (Zhengzhou Shengyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., China). All pH values were acquired on a FE-20 K pH-meter (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland)

The CQDs were synthesized via ultrasonic method according to our previous report [21]. In brief, 10 mL glucose aqueous solution (1 M) was mixed with 10 mL NaOH solution (1.5 M) followed by sonicate at 400-watt power for 4 h to get the raw solution of CQDs. After the pH value of the mixture solution was adjusted to 7 with HCl, 100 mL absolute ethyl alcohol was added dropwise with stirring. Then, the solution of CQDs was treated by adding a moderate amount of magnesium sulfate (12 wt.%) and stirring for 20 min to remove excess salt.

The DA aptamers (single-stranded DNA) were bound to AuNPs through Au-S interactions. [22–24]. First, 10 μM of aptamer was activated with 10 mM TCEP for 2 h in Tris−HCl buffer. Then 30 μL activated DNA was added to100 μL AuNPs solution and the mixture was shaken gently at 37℃ for 16 h. After a centrifugation for 20 min at 14,000 rpm, the mixture was washed three times with 200 μL PBS. Finally, the conjugates of aptamer-AuNPs was resuspended in 200 μL PBS and stored at 4℃ for use.

To begin with, 200 μL of aforementioned aptamer-AuNPs was added into 250 μL CQDs solution to quench the fluorescence. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction solution was measured. Afterwards, 200 μL of DA solution of different concentrations (0.05, 25, 100, 150, 200, 250 μM) was added into the reaction solution. Then the mixture was incubated for 3 min at room temperature before the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the effect of the sensor in real sample, serum samples (diluted 10 folds with PBS) mixed with various concentrations of DA, and the determination of DA in human serum sample was performed as described in Section 2.5.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs(a), CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer(b), and CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA(c) are shown in Fig.1 . The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was decreased with the addition of AuNPs-aptamer. When DA was added, the DA specifically binding to the aptamer. Thus, the CQDs would detach from the surface of AuNPs, and the fluorescence intensity was significantly recovered. So the detection of DA was realized according to the fluorescent signal changes of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA system.

The proper concentration ratio of aptamer to AuNPs is very important. When the aptamer concentration is too large, the aptamers combined with AuNPs will be folding and intertwining. On the other hand, the binding of low concentration target only bring negligible mass change of aptamer, so the produced fluorescence changes will be mild in a high noise background. To optimize the content of aptamer, the volumes of aptamer (10 μM) were set as 5, 20, 30 and 45 μL, respectively, when the AuNPs volumes were fixed at 100 μL. Then, their fluorescence spectra were detected by adding carbon quantum dots and dopamine at the same volume and concentration, as shown in Fig.2 . The peak fluorescence intensities increased with the increase of aptamer volumes, and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum when the aptamer volume was 30 μL. When the volume of aptamer was over 30 μL, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced. The reason may be that the distance between the carbon quantum dots is too close due to the folding and intertwining of aptamers, and the concentration quenching phenomenon occurs [20]. Thus, the volume and concentration of aptamer was selected as 30 μL, 10 μM in the further experiments.

Meanwhile, in order to obtain the smallest background signal, the content of CQDs should be optimized. In this experiment, different volumes of CQDs (with the same concentration) were added to the prepared 200 μL AuNPs-aptamer solutions. And the fluorescence spectra of and CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer were measured in Fig.3 . It showed that with the increase of the CQDs volumes, the fluorescence peak intensities first increased and then decreased. The fluorescence intensity reached a minimum when CQDs volume at 250 μL, and remained stable. This is because when the CQDs are added too little, the distance between CQDs and AuNPs is too far, and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer is not easy to occur [22,25]. With the increase of CQDs, most CQDs will combine with the aptamers through hydrogen bonds. CQDs near AuNPs will have resonance energy transfer with AuNPs and reduce their fluorescence intensity. According to the detection mechanism of this method, the more the fluorescence intensity decreases, and the more the fluorescence can be restored after the addition of DA. Therefore, 250 μL of CQDs was selected for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 4 (a) showed the fluorescence spectra of CQDs/AuNPs-aptamer/DA system with different concentrations (0.05, 25, 100, 150, 200, 250 μM) of DA. When DA was added, the DA bound specifically to its aptamer leading to the recovery of CQDs fluorescence intensity. And the fluorescence intensity in the solution increased along with the increasing of DA. Fig. 4 (b) revealed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and dopamine concentration in the range of 0.05˜250 μM. The linear regression equation was Y = 2.331 + 2.722 × 10-4X (Y was the ratio of fluorescence recovery intensity (F) to fluorescence quenching intensity (F0), X was the dopamine concentration). The regression coefficient is 0.991, and the detection limit (LOD) for DA was calculated to be 0.01 μM (3 times the standard deviation above the blank divided by the slope of the calibration curve).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of DA based on the assay
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is one of the important parameters for evaluating the performance of a new fluorescent aptasensor. So a variety of interferences (25 μM) were tested by the constructed aptasensor, including Na+, UA, urea, glucose, Mg2+, amino acid (AA), ATP and sucrose (Fig.5 ). The relative fluorescence intensity of the aptasensor toward DA (25 μM) was significantly more than other substances, which had proved the selectivity of the constructed aptasensor toward DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the practicability of the proposed method for DA detection in complex biological fluids, the developed sensing platform was applied to detect DA in human serum samples. Fig.6 (a) showed the fluorescence spectra of CQDs/aptamer-AuNPs/DA, when the concentrations of DA were 0.8、25、40、60 and 100 μM in human serum sample. Human serum was diluted 10 times with PBS solution. Fig. 6(b) revealed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and DA concentration in human serum sample in the range of 0.8˜100 μM, and the LOD was 0.8 μM.

In conclusion, a simple and label-free sensing platform was developed to recognize DA, based on gold nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots. The fluorescent aptasensor could effectively detect DA with a detection limit of 0.01 μM. Besides, the aptasensor was able to precisely detect DA in human serum sample. The specificity analysis experiment indicated that this method had obvious specificity for DA detection. This strategy is promising to be applied for detection of pathogenic bacterium in food safety and biomedical diagnostics by replacing the appropriate aptamers.

This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61801436), Science and Technology Innovation Outstanding Talents Project of Henan Province (Grant No. 184200510015), Henan Province Science and Technology Plan Project - International Cooperation Project (Grant No. 182102410093), Key research projects in colleges and universities of Henan Province (Grant No. 18A510021), Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing (HKLBRSK1801) and Graduate Technology Innovation Fund Project of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry (Grant No.2018022).

Linjiao Ren: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xinxin Hang: Data curation, Methodology. Zirui Qin: . Pei Zhang: Validation. Wei Wang: Project administration. Yintang Zhang: Resources. Liying Jiang: Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare no conflict of interest. All funders had no role in the design of the study; in the collection, analyses, or interpretation of data; in the writing of the manuscript, or in the decision to publish the results.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamers facilitating amplified detection of biomolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, F.; Zhang, H.; Wang, Z.; Newbigging, A. M.; Reid, M. S.; Li, X. F.; Le, X. C., Aptamers facilitating amplified detection of biomolecules. Anal Chem 2015, 87, (1), 274-92.
Nutiu, R.; Li, Y., Structure-switching signaling aptamers. Journal of the American Chemical Society 2003, 125, (16), 4771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A DNA aptamer with high affinity and specificity for therapeutic anthracyclines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wochner, A.; Menger, M.; Orgel, D.; Cech, B.; Rimmele, M.; Erdmann, V. A.; Glökler, J., A DNA aptamer with high affinity and specificity for therapeutic anthracyclines. Analytical Biochemistry 2008, 373, 34-42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The aptamer DNA-templated fluorescence silver nanoclusters: ATP detection and preliminary mechanism investigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, J.; Wei, C., The aptamer DNA-templated fluorescence silver nanoclusters: ATP detection and preliminary mechanism investigation. Biosensors & Bioelectronics 2017, 87, 422-427.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a FRET-based fluorescence aptasensor for the detection of aflatoxin B1 in contaminated food grain samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V, A. K. Y. V.; M, R. R.; J, A.; Mudili, V.; Poda, S., Development of a FRET-based fluorescence aptasensor for the detection of aflatoxin B1 in contaminated food grain samples. Rsc Advances 2018, 8, 10465-10473.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-sensitive detection of kanamycin for food safety using a reduced graphene oxide-based fluorescent aptasensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ha, N. R.; Jung, I. P.; La, I. J.; Jung, H. S.; Yoon, M. Y., Ultra-sensitive detection of kanamycin for food safety using a reduced graphene oxide-based fluorescent aptasensor. Scientific Reports 2017, 7, 40305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile aptamer-based sensing strategy for dopamine through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between rhodamine B and gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, J.; Li, Y.; Wang, L.; Huang, Y.; Liu, D.; Sun, R.; Luo, J.; Sun, C., A facile aptamer-based sensing strategy for dopamine through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between rhodamine B and gold nanoparticles. Dyes & Pigments 2015, 123, 55-63.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and selective fluorometric assay for dopamine using a calcein blue–Fe 2+ complex fluorophore

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Seto, D.; Maki, T.; Soh, N.; Nakano, K.; Ishimatsu, R.; Imato, T., A simple and selective fluorometric assay for dopamine using a calcein blue–Fe 2+ complex fluorophore. Talanta 2012, 94, 36-43.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Universal fluorometric aptasensor platform based on water-soluble conjugated polymers/graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo, L.; Hu, Y.; Zhang, Z.; Tang, Y., Universal fluorometric aptasensor platform based on water-soluble conjugated polymers/graphene oxide. Analytical & Bioanalytical Chemistry 2018, 410, (1), 287-295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-enhanced fluorescent probes based on silver nanoparticles and its application in IgE detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wei, X.; Li, H.; Li, Z.; Vuki, M.; Fan, Y.; Zhong, W.; Xu, D., Metal-enhanced fluorescent probes based on silver nanoparticles and its application in IgE detection. Analytical & Bioanalytical Chemistry 2012, 402, 1057-1063.

Food Analytical Methods 2016, 9, 1-10.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, R.; Lu, K. Q.; Tang, Z. R.; Xu, Y. J., Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis. Journal of Materials Chemistry A 2017, 5, 3717-3734.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient invitro and invivo pulmonary delivery of nucleic acid by carbon dot-based nanocarriers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pierrat, P.; Wang, R.; Kereselidze, D.; Lux, M.; Didier, P.; Kichler, A.; Pons, F.; Lebeau, L., Efficient invitro and invivo pulmonary delivery of nucleic acid by carbon dot-based nanocarriers. Biomaterials 2015, 51, 290-302.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Samantara, A. K.; Maji, S.; Ghosh, A.; Bag, B.; Dash, R.; Jena, B. K., Good's buffer derived highly emissive carbon quantum dots: excellent biocompatible anticancer drug carrier. Journal of Materials Chemistry B 2016, 4, 2412-2420.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abi-Dargham, A., A Dual Hit Model for Dopamine in Schizophrenia. Biological Psychiatry 2017, 81, 2-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of the binding constant of specific interactions and binding target concentration simultaneously using a general chemiluminescence method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao Lingfeng, Ju Li, Cui Hua. Determination of the binding constant of specific interactions and binding target concentration simultaneously using a general chemiluminescence method [J]. RSC Advances. 2016,6(7):5305-5311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influencing factors of luminescence properties of carbon dots prepared by ultrasonic

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ren, L. J.; Zhang, P.; Ru-Bin, Q. I.; Yin, J.; Liu, S.; Zhang, J. T.; Chen, Q. H.; Jiang, L. Y., Influencing Factors of Luminescence Properties of Carbon Dots Prepared by Ultrasonic. Spectroscopy & Spectral Analysis 2017, 37, 3354-3359.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Distance dependent fluorescence quenching and enhancement of gold nanoclusters by gold nanoparticles
Spectrochim. Acta A. Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qin, H.; Ma, D.; Du, J., Distance dependent fluorescence quenching and enhancement of gold nanoclusters by gold nanoparticles. Spectrochimica Acta Part A Molecular & Biomolecular Spectroscopy 2017, 189, 161-166.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ochratoxin A detection platform based on signal amplification by Exonuclease III and fluorescence quenching by gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Y.; Liu, R.; Sun, W.; Lv, L.; Guo, Z., Ochratoxin A detection platform based on signal amplification by Exonuclease III and fluorescence quenching by gold nanoparticles. Sensors & Actuators B Chemical 2017, 255, 1640-1645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybridization chain reaction coupled with the fluorescence quenching of gold nanoparticles for sensitive cancer protein detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Z.; Miao, X.; Cheng, Z.; Wang, P., Hybridization chain reaction coupled with the fluorescence quenching of gold nanoparticles for sensitive cancer protein detection. Sensors & Actuators B Chemical 2017, 243, 731-737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) for biosensors: general approaches and a review of recent developments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jeong, Y.; Kook, Y. M.; Lee, K.; Koh, W. G., Metal Enhanced Fluorescence (MEF) for Biosensors: General Approaches and A Review of Recent Developments. Biosensors & Bioelectronics 2018, 111, 102-116.
